  1809	    bool isBufferLoadCall(IRCall* inst)
  1810	    {
  1811	        // TODO: We should have something like a `[knownSemantics(...)]` decoration
  1812	        // that can identify that an `IRFunc` has semantics that are consistent
  1813	        // with a list of compiler-known behaviors. The operand to `knownSemantics`
  1814	        // could come from a `KnownSemantics` enumeration or something similar,
  1815	        // so that we don't have to make string-based checks here.
  1816	        //
  1817	        // Note that `[knownSemantics(...)]` would be independent of any targets,
  1818	        // and could even apply to functions that are implemented entirely in Slang.
  1819	
  1820	        if (auto targetIntrinsic = findAnyTargetIntrinsicDecoration(inst->getCallee()))
  1821	        {
  1822	            auto name = targetIntrinsic->getDefinition();
  1823	            if (name == ".operator[]" || name == ".Load" || name == ".Consume")
  1824	            {
  1825	                return true;
  1826	            }
  1827	        }
  1828	        return false;
  1829	    }
  1830	
  1831	    /// Used by `maybeSpecailizeBufferLoadCall`, this function returns a new specialized callee
  1832	    /// that replaces a `specialize(.operator[], oldType)` to `specialize(.operator[],
  1833	    /// newElementType)`.
  1834	    IRInst* getNewSpecializedBufferLoadCallee(
  1835	        IRInst* oldSpecializedCallee,
  1836	        IRType* newContainerType,
  1837	        IRType* newElementType)
  1838	    {
  1839	        auto oldSpecialize = cast<IRSpecialize>(oldSpecializedCallee);
  1840	        SLANG_ASSERT(oldSpecialize->getArgCount() == 1);
  1841	        IRBuilder builder(module);
  1842	        builder.setInsertBefore(oldSpecializedCallee);
  1843	        auto calleeType = builder.getFuncType(1, &newContainerType, newElementType);
  1844	        auto newSpecialize = builder.emitSpecializeInst(
  1845	            calleeType,
  1846	            oldSpecialize->getBase(),
  1847	            1,
  1848	            (IRInst**)&newElementType);
  1849	        return newSpecialize;
  1850	    }
  1851

2026-05-28 18:25:22,772 - INFO - swea-agent - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I understand the issue better. The `isBufferLoadCall` only matches `.operator[]`, `.Load`, and `.Consume` - but NOT `.Store`. Also, this only applies to structured buffers, not byte-address buffers. The `StructuredBuffer.Load<T>` and `RWStructuredBuffer.Load<T>` use `.operator[]` which gets handled. But `ByteAddressBuffer.Load<T>` and `.Store<T>` map to different intrinsic calls.

The problem is that `ByteAddressBuffer.Load<T>` and `.Store<T>` with an interface type `T` creates IR that contains `lookupWitness` and other existential-related instructions that never get lowered/specialized before the final code emit.

Let me look at what `__byteAddressBufferLoad` and `__byteAddressBufferStore` look like in the IR:







🎬 ACTION
cd /testbed && cat > /tmp/test_debug.slang << 'EOF'
[anyValueSize(8)]
interface IFoo
{
    float getValue();
}

struct ImplA : IFoo
{
    float v;
    float getValue() { return v; }
}

RWByteAddressBuffer byteBuffer;
RWStructuredBuffer<float> outputBuffer;

[numthreads(1, 1, 1)]
void computeMain()
{
    ImplA a;
    a.v = 7.0;
    IFoo fooA = a;
    byteBuffer.Store<IFoo>(0, fooA);

    IFoo loaded = byteBuffer.Load<IFoo>(0);
    outputBuffer[0] = loaded.getValue();
}
EOF

./build/Release/bin/slangc /tmp/test_debug.slang -target spirv -stage compute -entry computeMain -conformance "ImplA:IFoo=1" -dump-ir -o /dev/null 2>&1 | head -100

2026-05-28 18:25:40,240 - INFO - swea-agent - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I can see the issue clearly. In the IR dump:

1. `byteAddressBufferStore(%byteBuffer, 0 : UInt, 0 : UInt, %fooA)` - stores the existential value directly
2. `let %loaded : %IFoo = call specialize(%190, %IFoo)(%byteBuffer, 0 : UInt)` - loads the existential value directly

The load and store work with the `IFoo` interface type directly. These operations need to be legalized by the byte-address legalization pass. But the byte-address legalization pass doesn't handle interface types - it only handles struct, array, matrix, vector, and basic types.

When the type is an interface type (like `IFoo`), the `emitLegalLoad` function falls through to `emitSimpleLoad`, which then calls `getEquivalentStructuredBuffer` which creates a `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>`. But then the `StructuredBufferLoad` with interface type doesn't get properly lowered either because the `StructuredBufferLoad` with an interface type would need to go through existential lowering.

Let me trace through what happens more carefully. The `translateToStructuredBufferOps` option is true for SPIRV. So `emitSimpleLoad` will call `getEquivalentStructuredBuffer(type=IFoo, buffer=...)`. This creates a `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>`. Then it does `StructuredBufferLoad` on that, which returns an `IFoo` value. But then later passes need to lower the `lookupWitness` on that loaded `IFoo`, and they don't.

Wait, let me check whether `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` actually works. Let me try a simpler test

[anyValueSize(8)]
interface IFoo
{
    float getValue();
}

struct ImplA : IFoo
{
    float v;
    float getValue() { return v; }
}

RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo> structuredBuffer;
RWStructuredBuffer<float> outputBuffer;

[numthreads(1, 1, 1)]
void computeMain()
{
    ImplA a;
    a.v = 7.0;
    IFoo fooA = a;
    structuredBuffer[0] = fooA;

    IFoo loaded = structuredBuffer[0];
    outputBuffer[0] = loaded.getValue();
}
EOF

  2919	        auto baseType = type->getBaseType();
  2920	        UInt slotOperandCount = type->getExistentialArgCount();
  2921	
  2922	        IRBuilder builder(module);
  2923	        builder.setInsertBefore(type);
  2924	
  2925	        if (auto baseInterfaceType = as<IRInterfaceType>(baseType))
  2926	        {
  2927	            // We always expect two slot operands, one for the concrete type
  2928	            // and one for the witness table.
  2929	            //
  2930	            SLANG_ASSERT(slotOperandCount == 2);
  2931	            if (slotOperandCount < 2)
  2932	                return false;
  2933	
  2934	            auto concreteType = (IRType*)type->getExistentialArg(0);
  2935	            auto witnessTable = type->getExistentialArg(1);
  2936	            auto newVal =
  2937	                builder.getBoundInterfaceType(baseInterfaceType, concreteType, witnessTable);
  2938	
  2939	            addUsersToWorkList(type);
  2940	            type->replaceUsesWith(newVal);
  2941	            type->removeAndDeallocate();
  2942	            return true;
  2943	        }
  2944	        else if (
  2945	            as<IRPointerLikeType>(baseType) || as<IRHLSLStructuredBufferTypeBase>(baseType) ||
  2946	            as<IRArrayTypeBase>(baseType) || as<IRAttributedType>(baseType))
  2947	        {
  2948	            // A `BindExistentials<P<T>, ...>` can be simplified to
  2949	            // `P<BindExistentials<T, ...>>` when `P` is a pointer-like
  2950	            // type constructor.
  2951	            //
  2952	            IRType* baseElementType = nullptr;
  2953	            if (auto basePtrLikeType = as<IRPointerLikeType>(baseType))
  2954	                baseElementType = basePtrLikeType->getElementType();
  2955	            else if (auto arrayType = as<IRArrayTypeBase>(baseType))
  2956	                baseElementType = arrayType->getElementType();
  2957	            else if (auto baseSBType = as<IRHLSLStructuredBufferTypeBase>(baseType))
  2958	                baseElementType = baseSBType->getElementType();
  2959	            else if (auto baseAttrType = as<IRAttributedType>(baseType))
  2960	                baseElementType = baseAttrType->getBaseType();
  2961	
  2962	            IRInst* wrappedElementType = builder.getBindExistentialsType(
  2963	                baseElementType,
  2964	                slotOperandCount,
  2965	                type->getExistentialArgs());
  2966	            // Create a new type inst where the first operand is replaced
  2967	            // with wrappedElementType.
  2968	            ShortList<IRInst*> operands;
  2969	            operands.add(wrappedElementType);
  2970	            for (UInt i = 1; i < baseType->getOperandCount(); i++)

Now I see. The `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` works because there's a pass (`maybeSpecializeBindExistentialsType`) that transforms `BindExistentials<StructuredBuffer<T>, ...>` into `StructuredBuffer<BindExistentials<T, ...>>`. And later, `StructuredBufferLoad` with the element type being `BindExistentials<T, ...>` is handled by the `maybeSpecializeBufferLoadCall` which transforms `Load(wrapExistential(...))` into `wrapExistential(Load(...))`.

But for `ByteAddressBuffer`, the byte-address legalization pass converts `ByteAddressBufferLoad(IFoo)` into `StructuredBufferLoad(StructuredBuffer<IFoo>)` (when `translateToStructuredBufferOps` is true). The problem is that this conversion creates a `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` type which is then used in `StructuredBufferLoad`. But the `StructuredBufferLoad` with an existential element type (like `IFoo`) would need to be further processed by the existential specialization pass.

Wait, but the StructuredBuffer test case works fine. Let me check the difference. In the ByteAddressBuffer case, the byte-address legalization pass runs. Let me check what it produces and then what passes run after it.

Actually, let me look at the flow more carefully. The byte-address legalization pass converts:

```
byteAddressBufferStore(%byteBuffer, 0, 0, %fooA)  // where %fooA is IFoo
```

Into something like:

```
structuredBufferStore(getEquivalentStructuredBuffer(IFoo, %byteBuffer), offset/stride, %fooA)
```

And for load:

```
byteAddressBufferLoad(%byteBuffer, 0, 0) -> IFoo
```

Into:

```
structuredBufferLoad(getEquivalentStructuredBuffer(IFoo, %byteBuffer), offset/stride) -> IFoo
```

Now, when the type is `IFoo` (an interface), the `StructuredBufferLoad` would produce an `IFoo` value. This value is then used with `lookupWitness` etc. The problem is that for `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` to work correctly, the element type must go through `BindExistentials` first.

Let me check if the byte-address legalization happens before or after the existential specialization pass



🎬 ACTION

   982	    // When there are top-level existential-type parameters
   983	    // to the shader, we need to take the side-band information
   984	    // on how the existential "slots" were bound to concrete
   985	    // types, and use it to introduce additional explicit
   986	    // shader parameters for those slots, to be wired up to
   987	    // use sites.
   988	    //
   989	    if (requiredLoweringPassSet.bindExistential)
   990	        SLANG_PASS(bindExistentialSlots, sink);
   991	    validateIRModuleIfEnabled(codeGenContext, irModule);
   992	
   993	    // Now that we've linked the IR code, any layout/binding
   994	    // information has been attached to shader parameters
   995	    // and entry points. Now we are safe to make transformations
   996	    // that might move code without worrying about losing
   997	    // the connection between a parameter and its layout.
   998	
   999	    // One example of a transformation that needs to wait until
  1000	    // we have layout information is the step where we collect
  1001	    // any global-scope shader parameters with ordinary/uniform
  1002	    // type into an aggregate `struct`, and then (optionally)
  1003	    // wrap that `struct` up in a constant buffer.
  1004	    //
  1005	    // This step allows shaders to declare parameters of ordinary
  1006	    // type as globals in the input file, while ensuring that
  1007	    // downstream passes for graphics APIs like Vulkan and D3D
  1008	    // can assume that all ordinary/uniform data is strictly
  1009	    // passed using constant buffers.
  1010	    //

Now I can see the pass ordering. The byte-address legalization happens at line 1789-1794, which is after `specializeModule` (line 1181) and other specialization passes. 

The issue is: `bindExistentialSlots` runs at line 990, and `specializeModule` runs at line 1181. But `legalizeByteAddressBufferOps` runs at line 1790. So the existential binding/specialization happens BEFORE the byte-address legalization. 

When the byte-address legalization converts `ByteAddressBufferLoad<IFoo>` into `StructuredBufferLoad<StructuredBuffer<IFoo>>`, the resulting `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` type hasn't been through the existential specialization passes (because they already ran). So the existential type is left in the IR, and the later emit phases can't handle it.

Now let me verify this by looking at what happens when we use `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` directly. In that case, the original code already has `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>`, and the `bindExistentialSlots` and `specializeModule` passes handle it correctly before emit.

So the fix should be one of:
1. Make the byte-address legalization pass run BEFORE the existential specialization, or
2. Make the byte-address legalization pass handle interface types properly by checking if the type is an interface/existential and handling it accordingly (maybe by unpacking the existential into its components), or
3. Make the byte-address legalization pass detect interface types and produce a diagnostic error

Let me check if there's a way to handle this. First, let me check if the issue is indeed that `translateToStructuredBufferOps` produces `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` that doesn't get specialized.

Actually, looking at the PR description again: "Note: `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` and `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` work correctly with interface types. The issue is specific to the `ByteAddressBuffer` generic Load/Store path."

So the fix could be to make `ByteAddressBuffer.Load<T>` and `.Store<T>` work by converting them to equivalent `StructuredBuffer<T>` operations BEFORE the existential specialization runs. But the current order has byte-address legalization AFTER specialization.

Wait, let me look at the pass order more carefully. The `bindExistentialSlots` runs at 990, and `specializeModule` at 1181. The byte-address legalization is at 1790. But maybe we should move the byte-address legalization earlier?

Actually, looking at another approach: maybe we should check in the byte-address legalization whether the element type is an interface type, and if so, skip the legalization and leave the byte-address operations in place? But that would also fail because the backends don't support byte-address operations either.

  1685	    // For all targets, we translate load/store operations
  1686	    // of aggregate types from/to byte-address buffers into
  1687	    // stores of individual scalar or vector values.
  1688	    //
  1689	    if (requiredLoweringPassSet.byteAddressBuffer)
  1690	    {
  1691	        ByteAddressBufferLegalizationOptions byteAddressBufferOptions;
  1692	
  1693	        // Depending on the target, we may decide to do
  1694	        // more aggressive translation that reduces the
  1695	        // load/store operations down to invididual scalars
  1696	        // (splitting up vector ops).
  1697	        //
  1698	        switch (target)
  1699	        {
  1700	        default:
  1701	            break;
  1702	
  1703	        case CodeGenTarget::GLSL:
  1704	        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRV:
  1705	        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRVAssembly:
  1706	            // For GLSL targets, we want to translate the vector load/store
  1707	            // operations into scalar ops. This is in part as a simplification,
  1708	            // but it also ensures that our generated code respects the lax
  1709	            // alignment rules for D3D byte-address buffers (the base address
  1710	            // of a buffer need not be more than 4-byte aligned, and loads
  1711	            // of vectors need only be aligned based on their element type).
  1712	            //
  1713	            // Slang IR supports a variant of `Load<T>` on byte-address buffers
  1714	            // that will have greater alignment than required by D3D. The
  1715	            // alignment information is inferred from the operation like a
  1716	            // `Load4Aligned<T>` that returns a `vector<4,T>` that assumes a
  1717	            // `4*sizeof(T)` alignment. We may choose to disable that in favor
  1718	            // of byte-address indexing by setting this flag to true.
  1719	            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = false;
  1720	
  1721	            // For GLSL targets, there really isn't a low-level concept
  1722	            // of a byte-address buffer at all, and the standard "shader storage
  1723	            // buffer" (SSBO) feature is a lot closer to an HLSL structured
  1724	            // buffer for our purposes.
  1725	            //
  1726	            // In particular, each SSBO can only have a single element type,
  1727	            // so that even with bitcasts we can't have a single buffer declaration
  1728	            // (e.g., one with `uint` elements) service all load/store operations
  1729	            // (e.g., a `half` value can't be stored atomically if there are
  1730	            // `uint` elements, unless we use explicit atomics).
  1731	            //
  1732	            // In order to simplify things, we will translate byte-address buffer
  1733	            // ops to equivalent structured-buffer ops for GLSL targets, where
  1734	            // each unique type being loaded/stored yields a different global
  1735	            // parameter declaration of the buffer.
  1736	            //
  1737	            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = true;
  1738	            break;
  1739	        case CodeGenTarget::Metal:
  1740	        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLib:
  1741	        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLibAssembly:
  1742	            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
  1743	            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
  1744	            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
  1745	            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
  1746	            break;
  1747	        case CodeGenTarget::WGSL:
  1748	        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRV:
  1749	        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRVAssembly:
  1750	            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
  1751	            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
  1752	            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
  1753	            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
  1754	            byteAddressBufferOptions.useBitCastFromUInt = true;
  1755	            break;
  1756	        }
  1757	
  1758	        // We also need to decide whether to translate
  1759	        // any "leaf" load/store operations over to
  1760	        // use only unsigned-integer types and then
  1761	        // bit-cast, or if we prefer to leave them
  1762	        // as load/store of the original type.
  1763	        //
  1764	        switch (target)
  1765	        {
  1766	        case CodeGenTarget::HLSL:
  1767	            {
  1768	                auto profile = codeGenContext->getTargetProgram()->getOptionSet().getProfile();
  1769	                if (profile.getFamily() == ProfileFamily::DX)
  1770	                {
  1771	                    if (profile.getVersion() <= ProfileVersion::DX_5_0)
  1772	                    {
  1773	                        // Fxc and earlier dxc versions do not support
  1774	                        // a templates `.Load<T>` operation on byte-address
  1775	                        // buffers, and instead need us to emit separate
  1776	                        // `uint` loads and then bit-cast over to
  1777	                        // the correct type.
  1778	                        //
  1779	                        byteAddressBufferOptions.useBitCastFromUInt = true;
  1780	                    }
  1781	                }
  1782	            }
  1783	            break;
  1784	
  1785	        default:
  1786	            break;
  1787	        }
  1788	
  1789	        SLANG_PASS(
  1790	            legalizeByteAddressBufferOps,
  1791	            session,
  1792	            targetProgram,
  1793	            codeGenContext->getSink(),
  1794	            byteAddressBufferOptions);
  1795	    }
  1796

The `bindExistentialSlots` pass works on global parameters. If the byte-address legalization pass creates new `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` global parameters, they would need to go through `bindExistentialSlots` too.\n\nActually, let me check if we could just move the byte-address legalization earlier. Let me check if there\'s any dependency that prevents that.\n\nActually, looking at the code flow: the byte-address legalization pass at line 1790 comes after many other passes including:\n- `bindExistentialSlots` (990)\n- `specializeModule` (1181)\n\nBut it\'s before `specializeFuncsForBufferLoadArgs` (2128). So there might be some ordering constraints.\n\nLet me consider another approach: instead of moving the pass, we could handle interface types directly in the byte-address legalization pass. When we encounter an interface type (like `IFoo`), we could:\n1. Extract the existential value\'s components (the concrete value and witness table)\n2. I see. For HLSL target, `translateToStructuredBufferOps` is `false` (since it's not set in the HLSL case - only scalarize etc.). So for HLSL, the byte-address legalization pass tries to break down the load/store into scalars/vectors. But for interface types, it falls through to `emitSimpleLoad` which just emits the byte-address buffer load directly (since `translateToStructuredBufferOps` is false). Then the HLSL emit encounters this and fails because it can't handle interface types in its emit for `ByteAddressBufferLoad`.

For SPIRV/GLSL, `translateToStructuredBufferOps` is true, so it converts to `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` and then does `StructuredBufferLoad`. But the existential specialization has already run, so the `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` type is not further specialized, and the spirv emit fails on the `lookupWitness` instruction.

So the fix needs to handle interface types in both paths. The best approach would be to detect interface types (or more generally, types that contain existential/interface types) in the byte-address legalization pass and either:
1. Produce a clear diagnostic that they are not supported, or
2. Properly handle them by unpacking the existential into its components

Given the PR description says "The code should either compile and produce correct output (Load/Store serializes the existential tuple), or produce a clear diagnostic if interface types are not supported", let me look at how to implement the correct behavior.

Let me look at how the `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` case works to understand how to properly support interface types. The key is that `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` gets specialized by `bindExistentialSlots` and later passes that handle the `wrapExistential`/`bindExistential` wrapping.

Actually, wait. Let me re-examine. The `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` works because:
1. The declaration `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` is a global parameter
2. The `bindExistentialSlots` pass transforms it
3. The specialization pass handles it

But the `ByteAddressBuffer.Load<IFoo>` case creates a `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` on-the-fly during byte-address legalization, which happens AFTER those passes. So the solution might be to move the byte-address legalization EARLIER, or to re-run some of the existential passes after byte-address legalization.

But wait, let me look at this more carefully. The `bindExistentialSlots` pass works on global parameters. If the byte-address legalization pass creates new `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` global parameters, they would need to go through `bindExistentialSlots` too.

Actually, let me check if we could just move the byte-address legalization earlier. Let me check if there's any dependency that prevents that.

Actually, looking at the code flow: the byte-address legalization pass at line 1790 comes after many other passes including:
- `bindExistentialSlots` (990)
- `specializeModule` (1181)

But it's before `specializeFuncsForBufferLoadArgs` (2128). So there might be some ordering constraints.

Let me consider another approach: instead of moving the pass, we could handle interface types directly in the byte-address legalization pass. When we encounter an interface type (like `IFoo`), we could:
1. Extract the existential value's components (the concrete value and witness table)
2. Store/load those components individually as raw bytes
3. Reconstruct the existential on load

This is essentially what the `IRAnyValueMarshalling` infrastructure does. But that might be complex.

A simpler approach that matches the PR description would be to just emit a clear diagnostic when interface types are used with ByteAddressBuffer Load/Store. Let me check if there's a way to detect this and produce a diagnostic.

Actually, the most appropriate fix based on the PR description ("The code should either compile and produce correct output (Load/Store serializes the existential tuple), or produce a clear diagnostic if interface types are not supported") might be to produce a diagnostic. But to make it work correctly, we should handle the existential type properly.

So `specializeModule` doesn't handle ByteAddressBuffer operations. That means if we move byte-address legalization before specialization, the specialization pass won't be affected.

So we'd need to move byte-address legalization before `bindExistentialSlots`.

But wait, there's another issue. Let me check what the `collectGlobalUniformParameters` pass (line 1011) does and if it needs to run after byte-address legalization. Actually, byte-address legalization might create new global parameters that need layout information. The layout is attached at line 1011 (`collectGlobalUniformParameters`). If we run byte-address legalization before that, the new globals won't have layout yet. But looking at `createEquivalentStructuredBufferParam`, it copies the layout from the original buffer:

```cpp
if (auto layoutDecoration = byteAddressBufferParam->findDecoration<IRLayoutDecoration>())
{
    paramBuilder.addLayoutDecoration(structuredBufferParam, layoutDecoration->getLayout());
}
```

So the layout is copied from the original. This should work even before `collectGlobalUniformParameters`.

Actually, looking at the code more carefully, I think moving the byte-address legalization earlier might cause other issues. Let me look at what happens for the HLSL case - there, `translateToStructuredBufferOps` is false, so the pass doesn't create new structured buffer globals. The issue for HLSL is different: it tries to emit byte-address load/store operations with an interface type, and the HLSL emit can't handle that.

So for HLSL, the fix would need to be different. The byte-address legalization with `translateToStructuredBufferOps = false` falls through to `emitSimpleLoad`/`emitSimpleStore` which just emit the original byte-address buffer operations. If the type is an interface type, this produces an IR instruction that can't be emitted.

I think the proper fix is:
1. In `emitLegalLoad` and `emitLegalStore`, detect when the type is an interface type (or contains one) and produce a diagnostic error.

An interface type `IFoo` in the Slang IR is represented as a struct-like type with a witness table and a concrete value. The `anyValueSize` defines the size of the payload.

So the `generateAnyValueMarshallingFunctions` pass runs at line 1374. This is before byte-address legalization at 1790. So the marshalling functions are available.

But actually, looking at this differently, maybe the simplest approach is to handle interface types by treating them as a struct containing a witness table and a value, and then recursively legalizing the components. But the issue is that interface types aren't laid out like normal structs - they have a specific layout based on `anyValueSize`.

Let me look at the IR representation of interface types more carefully. In the Slang IR, `IFoo` is an `IRInterfaceType`. The `makeExistential` operation creates a value of type `IFoo` from a concrete value + witness table. And `extractExistentialValue`, `extractExistentialType`, `extractExistentialWitnessTable` extract the components.

So to properly store an `IFoo` value to a byte-address buffer, we need to:
1. Extract the witness table and concrete value
2. Store the witness table and concrete value separately (they have known sizes)

And to load, we need to:
1. Load the witness table and concrete value
2. Reconstruct the existential with `makeExistential`

But the concrete value's type is unknown at compile time - it depends on what concrete type implements the interface. The size of the concrete value is bounded by `anyValueSize`.

Hmm, this is getting complex. Let me look at how the existing code handles `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>`. The fact that it works means there's already infrastructure to handle interface types in buffers. Let me trace through what happens for `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>`:

1. User writes `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo> structuredBuffer;`
2. This creates a global parameter of type `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>`
3. `bindExistentialSlots` pass transforms this by adding bindings for the existential slots
4. `specializeModule` pass handles `structuredBuffer[0]` which returns `IFoo`
5. The `specializeModule` pass uses `maybeSpecializeBufferLoadCall` to transform `Load(wrapExistential(structuredBuffer, ...), idx)` into `wrapExistential(Load(structuredBuffer, idx), ...)`

Wait, but for `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>`, how does the actual load/store of the existential value work? It just loads/stores the existential as a blob of bytes equal to the `anyValueSize`. Let me check if there's special handling for this in the structured buffer load/store path.

Let me check `hlsl.meta.slang` for the StructuredBuffer Load/Store definitions

  1197	        //   let loadedVal : TaggedUnionType(...) = Load(tuPtr);

  1202	        //   let intermediateVal : InterfaceType(I) = Load(basePtr);
  1203	        //   let loadedTableID : TagType(tables) =

  1213	        // but the operands structure is slightly different.
  1214	        //
  1215	
  1216	        traverseUses(
  1217	            inst,
  1218	            [&](IRUse* use)
  1219	            {
  1220	                auto user = use->getUser();
  1221	                switch (user->getOp())
  1222	                {
  1223	                case kIROp_Load:
  1224	                    {
  1225	                        auto baseInterfacePtr = inst->getPtr();
  1226	                        auto baseInterfaceType = as<IRInterfaceType>(
  1227	                            as<IRPtrTypeBase>(baseInterfacePtr->getDataType())->getValueType());
  1228	
  1229	                        // Rewrite the load to use the original ptr and load
  1230	                        // an interface-typed object.
  1231	                        //
  1232	                        IRBuilder builder(module);
  1233	                        builder.setInsertAfter(user);
  1234	                        builder.replaceOperand(user->getOperands() + 0, baseInterfacePtr);
  1235	                        builder.replaceOperand(&user->typeUse, baseInterfaceType);
  1236	
  1237	                        // Then, we'll rewrite it.
  1238	                        List<IRUse*> oldUses;
  1239	                        traverseUses(user, [&](IRUse* oldUse) { oldUses.add(oldUse); });
  1240	
  1241	                        auto newVal = convertToTaggedUnion(
  1242	                            &builder,
  1243	                            user,
  1244	                            baseInterfaceType,
  1245	                            as<IRPtrTypeBase>(inst->getDataType())->getValueType());
  1246	                        for (auto oldUse : oldUses)
  1247	                        {
  1248	                            builder.replaceOperand(oldUse, newVal);
  1249	                        }
  1250	                        break;
  1251	                    }
  1252	                case kIROp_Store:
  1253	                    {
  1254	                        auto storeInst = cast<IRStore>(user);
  1255	
  1256	                        auto baseInterfacePtr = inst->getPtr();
  1257	                        auto baseInterfaceType = as<IRInterfaceType>(
  1258	                            as<IRPtrTypeBase>(baseInterfacePtr->getDataType())->getValueType());
  1259	
  1260	                        // Rewrite the store to use the original ptr and store
  1261	                        // an interface type'd object.
  1262	                        //
  1263	                        IRBuilder builder(module);
  1264	                        builder.setInsertBefore(user);
  1265	
  1266	                        auto newVal = convertToInterface(
  1267	                            &builder,
  1268	                            storeInst->getVal(),
  1269	                            baseInterfaceType,
  1270	                            as<IRPtrTypeBase>(inst->getDataType())->getValueType(),
  1271	                            witnessTableSet);
  1272	
  1273	                        builder.replaceOperand(storeInst->getPtrUse(), baseInterfacePtr);
  1274	                        builder.replaceOperand(storeInst->getValUse(), newVal);
  1275	                        break;
  1276	                    }
  1277	                case kIROp_StructuredBufferLoad:
  1278	                case kIROp_RWStructuredBufferLoad:
  1279	                    {
  1280	                        auto baseInterfacePtr = inst->getPtr();
  1281	                        auto baseInterfaceType =
  1282	                            as<IRInterfaceType>((baseInterfacePtr->getDataType())->getOperand(0));
  1283	
  1284	                        IRBuilder builder(module);
  1285	                        builder.setInsertAfter(user);
  1286	                        builder.replaceOperand(user->getOperands() + 0, baseInterfacePtr);
  1287	                        builder.replaceOperand(&user->typeUse, baseInterfaceType);
  1288	
  1289	                        // Then, we'll rewrite it.
  1290	                        List<IRUse*> oldUses;

So `lowerDynamicDispatchInsts` is called from `specializeModule` (via `typeflow-specialize`). So the handling of interface types in structured buffers happens during `specializeModule`, which runs at line 1181. The byte-address legalization at line 1790 runs AFTER that, so any `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` it creates won't get this processing.

Now I'm fairly confident about the root cause. The fix should ensure that when the byte-address legalization pass encounters an interface type, it handles it correctly.

Let me think about the best approach:

1. **Move byte-address legalization before specialization** - This would allow the `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` created by the legalization to be processed by the existential specialization passes. But this might have side effects and would require careful testing.

2. **Handle interface types directly in the byte-address legalization** - When we see an interface type, we can unpack it into its components (witness table + value) and store/load those separately. This is more self-contained.

3. **Emit a diagnostic** - Simply reject interface types in ByteAddressBuffer Load/Store.

Given the PR description says "The code should either compile and produce correct output (Load/Store serializes the existential tuple), or produce a clear diagnostic if interface types are not supported", option 3 would satisfy the requirement. But option 2 would be better for users.

Let me check if there's a simple way to implement option 2. The key insight is that an existential value like `IFoo` can be stored/loaded by:
1. Extracting the witness table and the concrete value
2. Storing/loading the witness table (which has a known size) and the concrete value (which has a size bounded by `anyValueSize`)
3. Reconstructing the existential from the loaded components

But wait, how does `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` actually work at the low level? Let me check: when `bindExistentialSlots` processes `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>`, what does it do?

Here's the result of running `cat -n` on /testbed/source/slang/slang-ir-bind-existentials.cpp:
   180	        }
   181	    }
   182	
   183	    // When processing a single parameter we need to have access
   184	    // to the corresponding instruction that will bind its slots.
   185	    //
   186	    // We don't care whether we have a `global_param` and a
   187	    // `bindGlobalExistentialSlots` instruction, or an entry-point
   188	    // function `param` and a `[bindExistentialSlots(...)]`
   189	    // decoration; both use the same subroutine.
   190	    //
   191	    void processEntryPointParameter(
   192	        IRInst* param,
   193	        IRInst* bindSlotsInst,
   194	        Index& ioSlotOperandOffset)
   195	    {
   196	        // We expect all shader parameters to have layout information,
   197	        // but to be defensive we will skip any that don't.
   198	        //
   199	        auto layoutDecoration = param->findDecoration<IRLayoutDecoration>();
   200	        if (!layoutDecoration)
   201	            return;
   202	        auto varLayout = as<IRVarLayout>(layoutDecoration->getLayout());
   203	        if (!varLayout)
   204	            return;
   205	
   206	        // We only care about parameters that are associated
   207	        // with one or more existential slots.
   208	        //
   209	        auto resInfo = varLayout->findOffsetAttr(LayoutResourceKind::ExistentialTypeParam);
   210	        if (!resInfo)
   211	            return;
   212	
   213	        // We will use the layout information on the variable to
   214	        // find out the stating slot, and the information on
   215	        // the type to find out the number of slots.
   216	        //
   217	        UInt slotCount = 0;
   218	        if (auto typeResInfo =
   219	                varLayout->getTypeLayout()->findSizeAttr(LayoutResourceKind::ExistentialTypeParam))
   220	            slotCount = typeResInfo->getFiniteSize();
   221	
   222	        // At this point we know that the parameter consumes
   223	        // some number of slots, so it would be an error
   224	        // if we don't have an instruction to bind the slots.
   225	        //
   226	        if (!bindSlotsInst)
   227	        {
   228	            // Note: This error is considered an internal error because
   229	            // we should be detecting and diagnosing this problem before
   230	            // we make it to back-end code generation.
   231	            //
   232	            sink->diagnose(
   233	                Diagnostics::MissingExistentialBindingsForParameter{.location = param->sourceLoc});
   234	            return;
   235	        }
   236	
   237	        // Each existential slot corresponds to *two* arguments
   238	        // on the binding instruction: one for the type, and
   239	        // another for the witness table.
   240	        //
   241	        // We will check to make sure we have enough operands to cover
   242	        // this parameter.
   243	        //
   244	        UInt bindOperandCount = bindSlotsInst->getOperandCount();
   245	        UInt slotOperandCount = 2 * slotCount;
   246	        if ((ioSlotOperandOffset + slotOperandCount) > bindOperandCount)
   247	        {
   248	            sink->diagnose(
   249	                Diagnostics::MissingExistentialBindingsForParameter{.location = param->sourceLoc});
   250	            return;
   251	        }
   252	        //
   253	        // If there are enough operands, then we will offset to
   254	        // get to the starting point for the current parameter,
   255	        // keeping in mind that each slot accounts for two
   256	        // operands.
   257	        //
   258	        auto operandsForInst = bindSlotsInst->getOperands() + ioSlotOperandOffset;
   259	
   260	        // Once we've found the operands that are relevent to
   261	        // the slots used by `param`, we will defer to a routine
   262	        // that replaces the type of `param` based on the
   263	        // information in the slots.
   264	        //
   265	        replaceTypeUsingExistentialSlots(param, slotOperandCount, operandsForInst);
   266	
   267	        ioSlotOperandOffset += slotOperandCount;
   268	    }
   269	
   270	    void replaceTypeUsingExistentialSlots(
   271	        IRInst* inst,
   272	        UInt slotOperandCount,
   273	        IRUse const* slotArgs)
   274	    {
   275	        // We are going to alter the type of the
   276	        // given `inst` based on information in
   277	        // the `slotArgs`.
   278	
   279	        auto fullType = inst->getFullType();
   280	
   281	        IRBuilder builder(module);
   282	
   283	        // Every argument that is filling an existential
   284	        // type param/slot comprises both a type and
   285	        // a witness table, so the total number of operands
   286	        // is twice the number of slots we are filling.
   287	        //
   288	        List<IRInst*> slotOperands;
   289	        for (UInt ii = 0; ii < slotOperandCount; ++ii)
   290	            slotOperands.add(slotArgs[ii].get());
   291	
   292	        // We are going to create a proxy type that represents
   293	        // the results of plugging all the information
   294	        // from the existential slots into the original type.
   295	        //
   296	        auto newType =
   297	            builder.getBindExistentialsType(fullType, slotOperandCount, slotOperands.getBuffer());
   298	
   299	        // We will replace the type of the original parameter
   300	        // with the new proxy type.

Now I understand the full picture. The `bindExistentialSlots` pass creates a `BindExistentials` type that wraps the original type (e.g., `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>`) with the concrete type and witness table information. Then `specializeModule` transforms operations on this wrapped type.

For example, `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` becomes `BindExistentials<RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>, ConcreteType, WitnessTable>`. Then when we do `structuredBuffer[0]`, the `maybeSpecializeBufferLoadCall` function in `specializeModule` transforms it from `Load(BindExistentials(structuredBuffer, ...), idx)` into `BindExistentials(Load(structuredBuffer, idx), ...)`. And eventually `BindExistentials<IFoo, ConcreteType, WitnessTable>` becomes a concrete interface type that can be lowered.

So for `ByteAddressBuffer.Load<IFoo>`, if we convert it to `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` before `bindExistentialSlots` runs, it should work. But currently byte-address legalization runs after.

   100	            // the packaged-up `struct` that we want to produce.
   101	            //
   102	            globalParamsTypeLayout =
   103	                globalParameterGroupTypeLayout->getElementVarLayout()->getTypeLayout();
   104	        }
   105	
   106	        // As a special case (in order to avoid disruption to any downstream passes),
   107	        // if the layout for the global-scope parameters doesn't include any "ordinary"
   108	        // data (represented as `LayoutResourceKind::Uniform`), then we will not do
   109	        // the "packaging up" step at all.
   110	        //
   111	        // This means that the current pass will not change the results for a majority
   112	        // of targets (notably, all the current graphics APIs) *unless* global shader
   113	        // parameters are declared that use "ordinary' data.
   114	        //
   115	        // TODO: eventually we should remove this special case, and confirm that the resulting
   116	        // logic works across all shaders (it should). Doing so will be a necessary
   117	        // step if want to start applying the packaging-up of global-scope parameters on
   118	        // a per-module basis.
   119	        //
   120	        if (!globalParameterGroupTypeLayout &&
   121	            !globalParamsTypeLayout->findSizeAttr(LayoutResourceKind::Uniform))
   122	            return;
   123	
   124	        // We expect that the layout for the global-scope parameters is always
   125	        // computed for a `struct` type.
   126	        //
   127	        auto globalParamsStructTypeLayout = as<IRStructTypeLayout>(globalParamsTypeLayout);
   128	        SLANG_ASSERT(globalParamsStructTypeLayout);
   129	
   130	        // We need to construct a single IR parameter that will represent
   131	        // the collected global-scope parameters. The `IRBuilder` we construct
   132	        // for this will also be used when replacing the individual parameters.
   133	        //
   134	
   135	        IRBuilder builderStorage(module);
   136	        IRBuilder* builder = &builderStorage;
   137	        builder->setInsertInto(module->getModuleInst());
   138	
   139	        // The packaged-up global parameters will be turned into fields of
   140	        // a new global IR `struct` type, which we give a name of `GlobalParams`
   141	        // so that it is identifiable in the output.
   142	        //
   143	        // Note: the equivalent in fxc/dxc is the `$Globals` constant buffer.
   144	        //
   145	        auto wrapperStructType = builder->createStructType();
   146	        builder->addNameHintDecoration(
   147	            wrapperStructType,
   148	            UnownedTerminatedStringSlice("GlobalParams"));
   149	        builder->addBinaryInterfaceTypeDecoration(wrapperStructType);
   150	
   151	        // If the computed layout used a bare `struct` type, then we will use
   152	        // our `GlobalParams` struct as-is, but if the layout involved an
   153	        // implicitly defined `ConstantBuffer<...>`, this is where we construct
   154	        // the type `ConstantBuffer<GlobalParams>`.
   155	        //
   156	        IRType* wrapperParamType = wrapperStructType;
   157	        if (globalParameterGroupTypeLayout)
   158	        {
   159	            auto wrapperParamGroupType = builder->getConstantBufferType(
   160	                wrapperStructType,
   161	                builder->getType(kIROp_DefaultBufferLayoutType));
   162	            wrapperParamType = wrapperParamGroupType;
   163	        }
   164	
   165	        // Now that we've determined what the type of the new single global parameter
   166	        // should be, we can go ahead and emit it into the IR module.
   167	        //
   168	        // We will call the implicit parameter for all the globals `globalParams`.
   169	        //
   170	        IRGlobalParam* wrapperParam = builder->createGlobalParam(wrapperParamType);
   171	        builder->addLayoutDecoration(wrapperParam, globalScopeVarLayout);
   172	        builder->addNameHintDecoration(wrapperParam, UnownedTerminatedStringSlice("globalParams"));
   173	
   174	        // With the setup work out of the way, we can iterate over the global
   175	        // parameters that were present in the layout information (they are
   176	        // represented as the fields of the global-scope `struct` layout).
   177	        //
   178	        // For CUDA targets, we need to ensure unsized arrays come last to satisfy
   179	        // the layout constraint in slang-ir-layout.cpp
   180	        auto fieldAttrs = globalParamsStructTypeLayout->getFieldLayoutAttrs();
   181	
   182	        // Create ordered field list - for CUDA, put unsized arrays last
   183	        List<IRStructFieldLayoutAttr*> orderedFields;
   184	
   185	        if (target == CodeGenTarget::CUDASource)
   186	        {
   187	            // For CUDA: separate regular and unsized array fields
   188	            List<IRStructFieldLayoutAttr*> regularFields;
   189	            List<IRStructFieldLayoutAttr*> unsizedArrayFields;
   190	
   191	            for (auto fieldLayoutAttr : fieldAttrs)
   192	            {
   193	                auto globalParam =
   194	                    _getGlobalParamFromLayoutFieldKey(fieldLayoutAttr->getFieldKey());
   195	                if (globalParam && as<IRUnsizedArrayType>(globalParam->getDataType()))
   196	                {
   197	                    unsizedArrayFields.add(fieldLayoutAttr);
   198	                }
   199	                else
   200	                {
   201	                    regularFields.add(fieldLayoutAttr);
   202	                }
   203	            }
   204	
   205	            // Add regular fields first, then unsized arrays
   206	            for (auto field : regularFields)
   207	                orderedFields.add(field);
   208	            for (auto field : unsizedArrayFields)
   209	                orderedFields.add(field);
   210	        }
   211	        else
   212	        {
   213	            // For other targets: preserve original order
   214	            for (auto field : fieldAttrs)
   215	                orderedFields.add(field);
   216	        }
   217	
   218	        for (auto fieldLayoutAttr : orderedFields)
   219	        {
   220	            // We expect the IR layout pass to have encoded field per-field
   221	            // layout so that the "key" for the field is the corresponding
   222	            // global shader parameter.
   223	
   224	            // Save the original global param before replacement.
   225	            auto globalParam = _getGlobalParamFromLayoutFieldKey(fieldLayoutAttr->getFieldKey());
   226	
   227	            auto globalParamLayout = fieldLayoutAttr->getLayout();
   228	
   229	            // Set insert position to a valid instruction under the global parent scope so we can
   230	            // create struct keys.
   231	            builder->setInsertAfter(fieldLayoutAttr->getFieldKey());
   232	
   233	            // This global parameter needs to be turned into a field of the global
   234	            // parameter structure type, and that field will need a key.
   235	            //
   236	            auto fieldKey = builder->createStructKey();
   237	
   238	            // In order to make sure that the existing IR layout information for
   239	            // the global scope remains valid, we will swap out the key in the
   240	            // per-field layout information to reference the key we created
   241	            // instead of the existing parameter (which we will be removing).
   242	            //
   243	            fieldLayoutAttr = as<IRStructFieldLayoutAttr>(
   244	                builder->replaceOperand(fieldLayoutAttr->getOperands(), fieldKey));
   245	
   246	            // If the given parameter doesn't contribute to uniform/ordinary usage, then
   247	            // we can safely leave it at the global scope and potentially avoid a lot
   248	            // of complications that might otherwise arise (that is, we don't need to worry
   249	            // about downstream passes that might have worked for a simple global parameter,
   250	            // but that would not work for one nested inside a structure.
   251	            //
   252	            // TODO: It would be more consistent and robust to *always* wrap up
   253	            // these global parameters appropriately, and ensure that all the downstream
   254	            // passes can handle that case, since they would need to do so in general.
   255	            //
   256	            if (!globalParamLayout->getTypeLayout()->findSizeAttr(LayoutResourceKind::Uniform))
   257	                continue;
   258	
   259	            SLANG_ASSERT(globalParam);
   260	
   261	            // The new structure field will need to have whatever decorations
   262	            // had been put on the global parameter (notably including any name hint)
   263	            //
   264	            globalParam->transferDecorationsTo(fieldKey);
   265	
   266	            // Now we can add a field to the `GlobalParams` type that
   267	            // will stand in for the parameter: it will have the key we
   268	            // just generated, and the type of the original parameter.
   269	            //
   270	            auto globalParamType = globalParam->getFullType();
   271	            builder->createStructField(wrapperStructType, fieldKey, globalParamType);
   272	
   273	            // Next we need to replace the uses of the parameter will
   274	            // logic to extract the appropriate field from the aggregated
   275	            // parameter.
   276	            //
   277	            // Unlike trivial cases that can work with `replaceAllUsesWith`,
   278	            // we are going to need to different code for each use, and that
   279	            // potentially puts us in the bad case of modifying the use-def
   280	            // information while also iterating it.
   281	            //
   282	            // To worka around the problem, we will make a copy of the list of
   283	            // uses and work with that instead.
   284	            //
   285	            List<IRUse*> uses;
   286	            for (auto use = globalParam->firstUse; use; use = use->nextUse)
   287	            {
   288	                uses.add(use);
   289	            }
   290	            for (auto use : uses)
   291	            {
   292	                auto user = use->user;
   293	
   294	                // There is an annoying gotcha here, in that we are using
   295	                // global shader parameters themselves (the `IRGlobalParam`s)
   296	                // to represent their "keys" in the layout objects that
   297	                // represent the layout of the global scope.
   298	                //
   299	                // We don't want to replace the reference to the global
   300	                // parameter in one of these layouts with a reference
   301	                // to a field of our new collected parameter, and instead
   302	                // want to replace such a reference with the key for that
   303	                // field.
   304	                //
   305	                // TODO: We should probably be assigning keys to global
   306	                // parameters, and using those keys in the layout instructions
   307	                // instead of directly using the parameters. The parameters
   308	                // could then have a decoration to assocaite them with their
   309	                // key.
   310	                //
   311	                // TODO: Alternatively, we could considering doing this
   312	                // kind of collection work earlier, on a per-module basis,
   313	                // so that we don't need to perform collection as a back-end step.
   314	                // (Note that the main sticking point there is explicit layout
   315	                // markers on global parameters, that stop the entire parameter
   316	                // range for a module from being contiguous).
   317	                //
   318	                if (auto layoutAttr = as<IRStructFieldLayoutAttr>(user))
   319	                {
   320	                    builder->replaceOperand(layoutAttr->getOperands(), fieldKey);
   321	                    continue;
   322	                }
   323	
   324	                // NumThreadsDecoration may sometimes be the user for a global
   325	                // parameter. This occurs when the parameter was supposed to be
   326	                // a specialization constant, but isn't due to that not being
   327	                // supported for the target. These can be skipped here and
   328	                // diagnosed later.
   329	                if (as<IRNumThreadsDecoration>(user))
   330	                {
   331	                    continue;
   332	                }
   333	
   334	                // For each use site for the global parameter, we will
   335	                // insert new code right before the instruction that uses
   336	                // the parameter.
   337	                //
   338	                // TODO: In some cases we might want to emit a single load of
   339	                // a global parameter at the start of a function, rather
   340	                // than individual loads at multiple points in the body
   341	                // of a function. Ideally we can/should annotate the
   342	                // `globalParams` parameter with the equivalent of `__restrict__`
   343	                // so that these loads can be merged/moved without concern
   344	                // for aliasing.
   345	                //
   346	                builder->setInsertBefore(user);
   347	
   348	                IRInst* value = nullptr;
   349	                if (globalParameterGroupTypeLayout)
   350	                {
   351	                    // If the global parameters are being placed in a
   352	                    // `ConstantBuffer<GlobalParams>`, then we need to
   353	                    // emit an instruction to compute a pointer to the
   354	                    // desired field, and then load from it.
   355	                    //
   356	                    auto ptrType = builder->getPtrType(globalParamType);
   357	                    auto fieldAddr = builder->emitFieldAddress(ptrType, wrapperParam, fieldKey);
   358	                    value = builder->emitLoad(globalParamType, fieldAddr);
   359	                }
   360	                else
   361	                {
   362	                    // If the global parameters are being bundled in a
   363	                    // plain old `struct`, then we simple want to emit
   364	                    // an instruction to extract the desired field.
   365	                    //
   366	                    value = builder->emitFieldExtract(globalParamType, wrapperParam, fieldKey);
   367	                }
   368	
   369	                // Whatever replacement value we computed, we need
   370	                // to install it as the value to be used at the use site.
   371	                //
   372	                use->set(value);
   373	            }
   374	
   375	            // Once we've replaced all uses of the global parameter,
   376	            // we can remove it from the IR module completely.
   377	            //
   378	            globalParam->removeAndDeallocate();
   379	        }
   380	    }
   381	};
   382	
   383	void collectGlobalUniformParameters(
   384	    IRModule* module,
   385	    IRVarLayout* globalScopeVarLayout,
   386	    CodeGenTarget target)
   387	{
   388	    CollectGlobalUniformParametersContext context;
   389	    context.module = module;
   390	    context.globalScopeVarLayout = globalScopeVarLayout;
   391	    context.target = target;
   392	
   393	    context.processModule();
   394	}
   395	
   396	} // namespace Slang

   980	
   981	
   982	    // When there are top-level existential-type parameters
   983	    // to the shader, we need to take the side-band information
   984	    // on how the existential "slots" were bound to concrete
   985	    // types, and use it to introduce additional explicit
   986	    // shader parameters for those slots, to be wired up to
   987	    // use sites.
   988	    //
   989	    if (requiredLoweringPassSet.bindExistential)
   990	        SLANG_PASS(bindExistentialSlots, sink);
   991	    validateIRModuleIfEnabled(codeGenContext, irModule);
   992	
   993	    // Now that we've linked the IR code, any layout/binding
   994	    // information has been attached to shader parameters
   995	    // and entry points. Now we are safe to make transformations
   996	    // that might move code without worrying about losing
   997	    // the connection between a parameter and its layout.
   998	
   999	    // One example of a transformation that needs to wait until
  1000	    // we have layout information is the step where we collect
  1001	    // any global-scope shader parameters with ordinary/uniform
  1002	    // type into an aggregate `struct`, and then (optionally)
  1003	    // wrap that `struct` up in a constant buffer.
  1004	    //
  1005	    // This step allows shaders to declare parameters of ordinary
  1006	    // type as globals in the input file, while ensuring that
  1007	    // downstream passes for graphics APIs like Vulkan and D3D
  1008	    // can assume that all ordinary/uniform data is strictly
  1009	    // passed using constant buffers.
  1010	    //
  1011	    SLANG_PASS(collectGlobalUniformParameters, outLinkedIR.globalScopeVarLayout, target);
  1012	    validateIRModuleIfEnabled(codeGenContext, irModule);
  1013	
  1014	    SLANG_PASS(checkEntryPointDecorations, target, sink);
  1015	
  1016	    // Add floating point denormal handling mode decorations to entry point functions based on
  1017	    // compiler options. This is done post-linking to ensure all entry points from linked
  1018	    // modules are processed.
  1019	    SLANG_PASS(addDenormalModeDecorations, codeGenContext);
  1020	    validateIRModuleIfEnabled(codeGenContext, irModule);

Now I need to understand: can I move the byte-address legalization to before line 989? The comment says "Now that we've linked the IR code, any layout/binding information has been attached to shader parameters and entry points." Let me try a different approach: instead of moving the existing pass, I can add the byte-address legalization both early (for the interface type case) and keep it late as well. But that would double-process.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The issue is specifically about interface types. When we have:

```
byteBuffer.Store<IFoo>(0, fooA);
```

This becomes a `byteAddressBufferStore` instruction. The byte-address legalization pass then:
1. For SPIRV/GLSL: converts it to `structuredBufferStore` with `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>`
2. For HLSL: falls through to emit `byteAddressBufferStore` directly

In both cases, the resulting IR contains instructions that the backend can't handle.

For SPIRV/GLSL, if we move byte-address legalization to before `bindExistentialSlots`, then the `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` would get processed by the existential specialization, and everything should work.

For HLSL, `translateToStructuredBufferOps` is false, so the byte-address legalization doesn't convert to structured buffer. Instead, it falls through to `emitSimpleLoad`/`emitSimpleStore` which just re-emit the original byte-address buffer operations. But the HLSL emit CAN handle `ByteAddressBufferLoad` and `ByteAddressBufferStore` (see line 1332 and 1395 in `slang-emit-hlsl.cpp`). The problem is that the HLSL emit tries to emit `Load<T>` where T is an interface type, and that fails because it tries to emit the type (`emitType(inst->getDataType())`) which might not work for interface types.

Wait, let me check the HLSL emit more carefully:

```cpp
case kIROp_ByteAddressBufferLoad:
    {
        // ...
        auto elementType = inst->getDataType();
        // ...
        if (elementType->getOp() == kIROp_UIntType)
        {
            // Use Load/Load2/Load3/Load4
            return true;
        }
        // Otherwise fall back to base case
        return false;
    }
```

The `return false` means it falls back to the base class `CLikeSourceEmitter` which does:

```cpp
case kIROp_ByteAddressBufferLoad:
    {
        m_writer->emit("(");
        emitOperand(inst->getOperand(0), getInfo(EmitOp::General));
        m_writer->emit(").Load<");
        emitType(inst->getDataType());
        m_writer->emit(" >(");
        emitOperand(inst->getOperand(1), getInfo(EmitOp::General));
        m_writer->emit(")");
        break;
    }
```

So it emits `.Load<IFoo>(...)`. But `IFoo` is an interface type, and `emitType` for an interface type might not work in HLSL. Actually, it might work if the type is defined in the generated code. Let me check...

Actually, let me just try to reproduce the HLSL error more carefully. The PR description says the HLSL target gives "internal error[E99999]: unimplemented feature in Slang compiler: unexpected IR opcode during code emit". This suggests some IR opcode is not handled in the HLSL emit.

Let me check if there's an `emitInterface` function that might be missing or if the HLSL backend can handle interface types at all.

Wait, let me re-read the error messages:
- SPIRV: "Unhandled local inst in spirv-emit: let %1 : %2 = lookupWitness(%3, %IFoox5FgetValue)"
- HLSL: "internal error[E99999]: unimplemented feature in Slang compiler: unexpected IR opcode during code emit"

For SPIRV, it's `lookupWitness` that's unhandled. For HLSL, it's some unexpected IR opcode. Both are because the existential types haven't been fully lowered.

So the root cause is the same: the byte-address legalization creates IR that contains existential operations (`lookupWitness`, etc.) that haven't been lowered because the existential specialization passes ran before the byte-address legalization.

The fix should be to ensure that after byte-address legalization, the IR doesn't contain interface types that need further lowering. There are two ways:
1. Move the byte-address legalization before existential specialization
2. Handle interface types properly in byte-address legalization itself

Let me try option 1 first, since it's simpler. I'll move the byte-address legalization to just before `bindExistentialSlots`.

But I need to be careful: the byte-address legalization pass uses `getNaturalSizeAndAlignment` which depends on the target. The target is available. It also uses `getEquivalentStructuredBuffer` which creates new global parameters. These new global parameters should be processed by `bindExistentialSlots` if they contain existential types.

Let me try this approach:

  1685	    // For all targets, we translate load/store operations
  1686	    // of aggregate types from/to byte-address buffers into
  1687	    // stores of individual scalar or vector values.
  1688	    //
  1689	    if (requiredLoweringPassSet.byteAddressBuffer)
  1690	    {
  1691	        ByteAddressBufferLegalizationOptions byteAddressBufferOptions;
  1692	
  1693	        // Depending on the target, we may decide to do
  1694	        // more aggressive translation that reduces the
  1695	        // load/store operations down to invididual scalars
  1696	        // (splitting up vector ops).
  1697	        //
  1698	        switch (target)
  1699	        {
  1700	        default:
  1701	            break;
  1702	
  1703	        case CodeGenTarget::GLSL:
  1704	        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRV:
  1705	        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRVAssembly:
  1706	            // For GLSL targets, we want to translate the vector load/store
  1707	            // operations into scalar ops. This is in part as a simplification,
  1708	            // but it also ensures that our generated code respects the lax
  1709	            // alignment rules for D3D byte-address buffers (the base address
  1710	            // of a buffer need not be more than 4-byte aligned, and loads
  1711	            // of vectors need only be aligned based on their element type).
  1712	            //
  1713	            // Slang IR supports a variant of `Load<T>` on byte-address buffers
  1714	            // that will have greater alignment than required by D3D. The
  1715	            // alignment information is inferred from the operation like a
  1716	            // `Load4Aligned<T>` that returns a `vector<4,T>` that assumes a
  1717	            // `4*sizeof(T)` alignment. We may choose to disable that in favor
  1718	            // of byte-address indexing by setting this flag to true.
  1719	            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = false;
  1720	
  1721	            // For GLSL targets, there really isn't a low-level concept
  1722	            // of a byte-address buffer at all, and the standard "shader storage
  1723	            // buffer" (SSBO) feature is a lot closer to an HLSL structured
  1724	            // buffer for our purposes.
  1725	            //
  1726	            // In particular, each SSBO can only have a single element type,
  1727	            // so that even with bitcasts we can't have a single buffer declaration
  1728	            // (e.g., one with `uint` elements) service all load/store operations
  1729	            // (e.g., a `half` value can't be stored atomically if there are
  1730	            // `uint` elements, unless we use explicit atomics).
  1731	            //
  1732	            // In order to simplify things, we will translate byte-address buffer
  1733	            // ops to equivalent structured-buffer ops for GLSL targets, where
  1734	            // each unique type being loaded/stored yields a different global
  1735	            // parameter declaration of the buffer.
  1736	            //
  1737	            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = true;
  1738	            break;
  1739	        case CodeGenTarget::Metal:
  1740	        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLib:
  1741	        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLibAssembly:
  1742	            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
  1743	            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
  1744	            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
  1745	            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
  1746	            break;
  1747	        case CodeGenTarget::WGSL:
  1748	        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRV:
  1749	        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRVAssembly:
  1750	            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
  1751	            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
  1752	            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
  1753	            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
  1754	            byteAddressBufferOptions.useBitCastFromUInt = true;
  1755	            break;
  1756	        }
  1757	
  1758	        // We also need to decide whether to translate
  1759	        // any "leaf" load/store operations over to
  1760	        // use only unsigned-integer types and then
  1761	        // bit-cast, or if we prefer to leave them
  1762	        // as load/store of the original type.
  1763	        //
  1764	        switch (target)
  1765	        {
  1766	        case CodeGenTarget::HLSL:
  1767	            {
  1768	                auto profile = codeGenContext->getTargetProgram()->getOptionSet().getProfile();
  1769	                if (profile.getFamily() == ProfileFamily::DX)
  1770	                {
  1771	                    if (profile.getVersion() <= ProfileVersion::DX_5_0)
  1772	                    {
  1773	                        // Fxc and earlier dxc versions do not support
  1774	                        // a templates `.Load<T>` operation on byte-address
  1775	                        // buffers, and instead need us to emit separate
  1776	                        // `uint` loads and then bit-cast over to
  1777	                        // the correct type.
  1778	                        //
  1779	                        byteAddressBufferOptions.useBitCastFromUInt = true;
  1780	                    }
  1781	                }
  1782	            }
  1783	            break;
  1784	
  1785	        default:
  1786	            break;
  1787	        }
  1788	
  1789	        SLANG_PASS(
  1790	            legalizeByteAddressBufferOps,
  1791	            session,
  1792	            targetProgram,
  1793	            codeGenContext->getSink(),
  1794	            byteAddressBufferOptions);
  1795	    }
  1796

2026-05-28 18:30:39,596 - DEBUG - swea-lm - Response: ModelResponse(id='f4621fcf3e4b4aecaf683c67c8951528', created=1779993025, model='DeepSeek-V4-Pro', object='chat.completion', system_fingerprint=None, choices=[Choices(finish_reason='tool_calls', index=0, message=Message(content="Now let me move this block to before `bindExistentialSlots`. 🎬 ACTION
str_replace_editor str_replace /testbed/source/slang/slang-emit.cpp   --old_str '// For all targets, we translate load/store operations
    // of aggregate types from/to byte-address buffers into
    // stores of individual scalar or vector values.
    //
    if (requiredLoweringPassSet.byteAddressBuffer)
    {
        ByteAddressBufferLegalizationOptions byteAddressBufferOptions;

        // Depending on the target, we may decide to do
        // more aggressive translation that reduces the
        // load/store operations down to invididual scalars
        // (splitting up vector ops).
        //
        switch (target)
        {
        default:
            break;

        case CodeGenTarget::GLSL:
        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRV:
        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRVAssembly:
            // For GLSL targets, we want to translate the vector load/store
            // operations into scalar ops. This is in part as a simplification,
            // but it also ensures that our generated code respects the lax
            // alignment rules for D3D byte-address buffers (the base address
            // of a buffer need not be more than 4-byte aligned, and loads
            // of vectors need only be aligned based on their element type).
            //
            // Slang IR supports a variant of `Load<T>` on byte-address buffers
            // that will have greater alignment than required by D3D. The
            // alignment information is inferred from the operation like a
            // `Load4Aligned<T>` that returns a `vector<4,T>` that assumes a
            // `4*sizeof(T)` alignment. We may choose to disable that in favor
            // of byte-address indexing by setting this flag to true.
            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = false;

            // For GLSL targets, there really isn'"'"'t a low-level concept
            // of a byte-address buffer at all, and the standard "shader storage
            // buffer" (SSBO) feature is a lot closer to an HLSL structured
            // buffer for our purposes.
            //
            // In particular, each SSBO can only have a single element type,
            // so that even with bitcasts we can'"'"'t have a single buffer declaration
            // (e.g., one with `uint` elements) service all load/store operations
            // (e.g., a `half` value can'"'"'t be stored atomically if there are
            // `uint` elements, unless we use explicit atomics).
            //
            // In order to simplify things, we will translate byte-address buffer
            // ops to equivalent structured-buffer ops for GLSL targets, where
            // each unique type being loaded/stored yields a different global
            // parameter declaration of the buffer.
            //
            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = true;
            break;
        case CodeGenTarget::Metal:
        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLib:
        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLibAssembly:
            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
            break;
        case CodeGenTarget::WGSL:
        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRV:
        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRVAssembly:
            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.useBitCastFromUInt = true;
            break;
        }

        // We also need to decide whether to translate
        // any "leaf" load/store operations over to
        // use only unsigned-integer types and then
        // bit-cast, or if we prefer to leave them
        // as load/store of the original type.
        //
        switch (target)
        {
        case CodeGenTarget::HLSL:
            {
                auto profile = codeGenContext->getTargetProgram()->getOptionSet().getProfile();
                if (profile.getFamily() == ProfileFamily::DX)
                {
                    if (profile.getVersion() <= ProfileVersion::DX_5_0)
                    {
                        // Fxc and earlier dxc versions do not support
                        // a templates `.Load<T>` operation on byte-address
                        // buffers, and instead need us to emit separate
                        // `uint` loads and then bit-cast over to
                        // the correct type.
                        //
                        byteAddressBufferOptions.useBitCastFromUInt = true;
                    }
                }
            }
            break;

        default:
            break;
        }

        SLANG_PASS(
            legalizeByteAddressBufferOps,
            session,
            targetProgram,
            codeGenContext->getSink(),
            byteAddressBufferOptions);
    }

    // For SPIR-V, this function is called elsewhere, so that it can happen after address space
    // specialization
    if (target != CodeGenTarget::SPIRV && target != CodeGenTarget::SPIRVAssembly)
    {
        bool skipFuncParamValidation = true;
        SLANG_PASS(validateAtomicOperations, skipFuncParamValidation, sink);
    }' --new_str '// For SPIR-V, this function is called elsewhere, so that it can happen after address space
    // specialization
    if (target != CodeGenTarget::SPIRV && target != CodeGenTarget::SPIRVAssembly)
    {
        bool skipFuncParamValidation = true;
        SLANG_PASS(validateAtomicOperations, skipFuncParamValidation, sink);
    }'
