    // of aggregate types from/to byte-address buffers into
    // stores of individual scalar or vector values.
    //
    // This pass needs to run before existential slot binding,
    // since it may create new StructuredBuffer<X> parameters
    // where X is an interface type, and those parameters need
    // to have their existential slots bound.
    //
    if (requiredLoweringPassSet.byteAddressBuffer)
    {
        ByteAddressBufferLegalizationOptions byteAddressBufferOptions;

        // Depending on the target, we may decide to do
        // more aggressive translation that reduces the
        // load/store operations down to invididual scalars
        // (splitting up vector ops).
        //
        switch (target)
        {
        default:
            break;

        case CodeGenTarget::GLSL:
        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRV:
        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRVAssembly:
            // For GLSL targets, we want to translate the vector load/store
            // operations into scalar ops. This is in part as a simplification,
            // but it also ensures that our generated code respects the lax
            // alignment rules for D3D byte-address buffers (the base address
            // of a buffer need not be more than 4-byte aligned, and loads
            // of vectors need only be aligned based on their element type).
            //
            // Slang IR supports a variant of `Load<T>` on byte-address buffers
            // that will have greater alignment than required by D3D. The
            // alignment information is inferred from the operation like a
            // `Load4Aligned<T>` that returns a `vector<4,T>` that assumes a
            // `4*sizeof(T)` alignment. We may choose to disable that in favor
            // of byte-address indexing by setting this flag to true.
            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = false;

            // For GLSL targets, there really isn'"'"'t a low-level concept
            // of a byte-address buffer at all, and the standard "shader storage
            // buffer" (SSBO) feature is a lot closer to an HLSL structured
            // buffer for our purposes.
            //
            // In particular, each SSBO can only have a single element type,
            // so that even with bitcasts we can'"'"'t have a single buffer declaration
            // (e.g., one with `uint` elements) service all load/store operations
            // (e.g., a `half` value can'"'"'t be stored atomically if there are
            // `uint` elements, unless we use explicit atomics).
            //
            // In order to simplify things, we will translate byte-address buffer
            // ops to equivalent structured-buffer ops for GLSL targets, where
            // each unique type being loaded/stored yields a different global
            // parameter declaration of the buffer.
            //
            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = true;
            break;
        case CodeGenTarget::Metal:
        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLib:
        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLibAssembly:
            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
            break;
        case CodeGenTarget::WGSL:
        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRV:
        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRVAssembly:
            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
            byteAddressBufferOptions.useBitCastFromUInt = true;
            break;
        }

        // We also need to decide whether to translate
        // any "leaf" load/store operations over to
        // use only unsigned-integer types and then
        // bit-cast, or if we prefer to leave them
        // as load/store of the original type.
        //
        switch (target)
        {
        case CodeGenTarget::HLSL:
            {
                auto profile = codeGenContext->getTargetProgram()->getOptionSet().getProfile();
                if (profile.getFamily() == ProfileFamily::DX)
                {
                    if (profile.getVersion() <= ProfileVersion::DX_5_0)
                    {
                        // Fxc and earlier dxc versions do not support
                        // a templates `.Load<T>` operation on byte-address
                        // buffers, and instead need us to emit separate
                        // `uint` loads and then bit-cast over to
                        // the correct type.
                        //
                        byteAddressBufferOptions.useBitCastFromUInt = true;
                    }
                }
            }
            break;

        default:
            break;
        }

        SLANG_PASS(
            legalizeByteAddressBufferOps,
            session,
            targetProgram,
            codeGenContext->getSink(),
            byteAddressBufferOptions);
    }

    // to the shader, we need to take the side-band information
    // on how the existential "slots" were bound to concrete

    // use sites.
    //
    if (requiredLoweringPassSet.bindExistential)
        SLANG_PASS(bindExistentialSlots, sink);

   977	    //
   978	    SLANG_PASS(replaceGlobalConstants);
   979	    validateIRModuleIfEnabled(codeGenContext, irModule);
   980	
   981	
   982	    // For all targets, we translate load/store operations
   983	    // of aggregate types from/to byte-address buffers into
   984	    // stores of individual scalar or vector values.
   985	    //
   986	    // This pass needs to run before existential slot binding,
   987	    // since it may create new StructuredBuffer<X> parameters
   988	    // where X is an interface type, and those parameters need
   989	    // to have their existential slots bound.
   990	    //
   991	    if (requiredLoweringPassSet.byteAddressBuffer)
   992	    {
   993	        ByteAddressBufferLegalizationOptions byteAddressBufferOptions;
   994	
   995	        // Depending on the target, we may decide to do
   996	        // more aggressive translation that reduces the
   997	        // load/store operations down to invididual scalars
   998	        // (splitting up vector ops).
   999	        //
  1000	        switch (target)
  1001	        {
  1002	        default:
  1003	            break;
  1004	
  1005	        case CodeGenTarget::GLSL:
  1006	        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRV:
  1007	        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRVAssembly:
  1008	            // For GLSL targets, we want to translate the vector load/store
  1009	            // operations into scalar ops. This is in part as a simplification,
  1010	            // but it also ensures that our generated code respects the lax
  1011	            // alignment rules for D3D byte-address buffers (the base address
  1012	            // of a buffer need not be more than 4-byte aligned, and loads
  1013	            // of vectors need only be aligned based on their element type).
  1014	            //
  1015	            // Slang IR supports a variant of `Load<T>` on byte-address buffers
  1016	            // that will have greater alignment than required by D3D. The
  1017	            // alignment information is inferred from the operation like a
  1018	            // `Load4Aligned<T>` that returns a `vector<4,T>` that assumes a
  1019	            // `4*sizeof(T)` alignment. We may choose to disable that in favor
  1020	            // of byte-address indexing by setting this flag to true.
  1021	            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = false;
  1022	
  1023	            // For GLSL targets, there really isn't a low-level concept
  1024	            // of a byte-address buffer at all, and the standard "shader storage
  1025	            // buffer" (SSBO) feature is a lot closer to an HLSL structured
  1026	            // buffer for our purposes.
  1027	            //
  1028	            // In particular, each SSBO can only have a single element type,
  1029	            // so that even with bitcasts we can't have a single buffer declaration
  1030	            // (e.g., one with `uint` elements) service all load/store operations
  1031	            // (e.g., a `half` value can't be stored atomically if there are
  1032	            // `uint` elements, unless we use explicit atomics).
  1033	            //
  1034	            // In order to simplify things, we will translate byte-address buffer
  1035	            // ops to equivalent structured-buffer ops for GLSL targets, where
  1036	            // each unique type being loaded/stored yields a different global
  1037	            // parameter declaration of the buffer.
  1038	            //
  1039	            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = true;
  1040	            break;
  1041	        case CodeGenTarget::Metal:
  1042	        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLib:
  1043	        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLibAssembly:
  1044	            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
  1045	            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
  1046	            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
  1047	            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
  1048	            break;
  1049	        case CodeGenTarget::WGSL:
  1050	        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRV:
  1051	        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRVAssembly:
  1052	            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
  1053	            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
  1054	            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
  1055	            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
  1056	            byteAddressBufferOptions.useBitCastFromUInt = true;
  1057	            break;
  1058	        }
  1059	
  1060	        // We also need to decide whether to translate
  1061	        // any "leaf" load/store operations over to
  1062	        // use only unsigned-integer types and then
  1063	        // bit-cast, or if we prefer to leave them
  1064	        // as load/store of the original type.
  1065	        //
  1066	        switch (target)
  1067	        {
  1068	        case CodeGenTarget::HLSL:
  1069	            {
  1070	                auto profile = codeGenContext->getTargetProgram()->getOptionSet().getProfile();
  1071	                if (profile.getFamily() == ProfileFamily::DX)
  1072	                {
  1073	                    if (profile.getVersion() <= ProfileVersion::DX_5_0)
  1074	                    {
  1075	                        // Fxc and earlier dxc versions do not support
  1076	                        // a templates `.Load<T>` operation on byte-address
  1077	                        // buffers, and instead need us to emit separate
  1078	                        // `uint` loads and then bit-cast over to
  1079	                        // the correct type.
  1080	                        //
  1081	                        byteAddressBufferOptions.useBitCastFromUInt = true;
  1082	                    }
  1083	                }
  1084	            }
  1085	            break;
  1086	
  1087	        default:
  1088	            break;
  1089	        }
  1090	
  1091	        SLANG_PASS(
  1092	            legalizeByteAddressBufferOps,
  1093	            session,
  1094	            targetProgram,
  1095	            codeGenContext->getSink(),
  1096	            byteAddressBufferOptions);
  1097	    }
  1098	
  1099	    // When there are top-level existential-type parameters
  1100	    // to the shader, we need to take the side-band information
  1101	    // on how the existential "slots" were bound to concrete
  1102	    // types, and use it to introduce additional explicit
  1103	    // shader parameters for those slots, to be wired up to
  1104	    // use sites.
  1105	    //
  1106	    if (requiredLoweringPassSet.bindExistential)
  1107	        SLANG_PASS(bindExistentialSlots, sink);
  1108	    validateIRModuleIfEnabled(codeGenContext, irModule);
  1109	
  1110	    // Now that we've linked the IR code, any layout/binding
  1111	    // information has been attached to shader parameters

Let me check if the crash is in `getEquivalentStructuredBuffer`. Maybe `getEquivalentStructuredBufferParamType` doesn't handle the case where the element type is an interface type. Let me trace through:

1. `emitSimpleStore` calls `getEquivalentStructuredBuffer(type, buffer)`
2. `getEquivalentStructuredBuffer` finds the global param `%byteBuffer` of type `RWByteAddressBuffer`
3. It calls `getEquivalentStructuredBufferParam(elementType=IFoo, byteAddressBufferParam)`
4. `createEquivalentStructuredBufferParam` calls `getEquivalentStructuredBufferParamType(elementType=IFoo, byteAddressBufferType=RWByteAddressBuffer)`
5. `getEquivalentStructuredBufferParamType` checks the op of `byteAddressBufferType` - it's `kIROp_HLSLRWByteAddressBufferType`
6. It returns `builder.getType(kIROp_HLSLRWStructuredBufferType, 2, typeOperands)` where `typeOperands` is `{IFoo, DefaultBufferLayout}`

This should create a `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` type. That should work.

This means an interface type can be stored as a blob of this size. The `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` path handles this by just storing/loading the blob. Interesting! The crash is in `IRBuilder::_findOrEmitHoistableInst` called from `fetchCompilerDictionaryEntry`. This is during `TypeFlowSpecializationContext::resolveTypesInFunc`.

Actually, looking at the stack trace: `TypeFlowSpecializationContext::resolveTypesInFunc` -> `TranslationContext::resolveInst` -> `_resolveInstRec` -> `IRBuilder::fetchCompilerDictionaryEntry`. This is happening during typeflow specialization. The issue might be that the byte-address legalization created an `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` that doesn't have its existential slots bound yet. But `bindExistentialSlots` runs after byte-address legalization (at line 1106), so the slots should be bound before typeflow specialization (which runs at line ~1280).

Wait, let me check the pass order again:

1. Line 1091-1096: byte-address legalization
2. Line 1106: `bindExistentialSlots`
3. Line 1280: `specializeModule` (which includes typeflow specialization)

So `bindExistentialSlots` should run before typeflow specialization. But the crash says it's in typeflow specialization, which means `bindExistentialSlots` didn't handle the new structured buffer params correctly.

Let me check: `bindExistentialSlots` works on entry point parameters. The new `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` created by byte-address legalization is a global parameter. Does `bindExistentialSlots` process global parameters?

Looking at the code in `slang-ir-bind-existentials.cpp`:

```cpp
void BindExistentialSlots::processModule()
{
    // We only apply to entry points...
    for (auto inst : module->getGlobalInsts())
    {
        auto func = as<IRFunc>(inst);
        if (!func)
            continue;
        // Find the [bindExistentialSlots(...)] decoration
        ...
    }
}
```

So `bindExistentialSlots` only processes entry point functions, not global parameters directly. But it does process the parameters OF entry points. The new `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` is a global parameter, not a parameter of an entry point. So it won't be processed.

But then how does `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` work when declared directly? Let me check...

When the user writes `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo> byteBuffer;` in global scope:
1. This becomes a global parameter `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>`
2. The front-end adds a `[bindExistentialSlots(...)]` decoration to the entry point
3. The `bindExistentialSlots` pass processes the entry point and its parameters

Actually, looking at the `bindExistentialSlots` code more carefully, it processes parameters of entry points. The global parameters that are used by an entry point are linked via the entry point's parameters. So if the global `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` is used in the entry point, it should be a parameter of the entry point.

But wait, the new `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` created by byte-address legalization is NOT a parameter of the entry point - it's a new global parameter that's used by the load/store instructions. It's not automatically added to the entry point's parameter list.

So the fix needs to also ensure that the new global parameter is properly linked to the entry point. Let me check how the original `RWByteAddressBuffer` parameter is linked: it's a global parameter that's directly referenced by the load/store instructions. The entry point references it indirectly through the load/store instructions. The `bindExistentialSlots` pass processes the entry point parameters, and the byte-address buffer should be one of them.

But wait, the `RWByteAddressBuffer` is a global parameter. Does it become a parameter of the entry point? Let me check...

In Slang, global shader parameters are NOT entry point parameters. They are global variables that are implicitly captured by the entry point. The `bindExistentialSlots` pass processes the entry point function's decorations to find `[bindExistentialSlots(...)]`. This decoration is placed by the front-end when there are existential types used in shader parameters.

But the new `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` created by byte-address legalization doesn't have such a decoration. So `bindExistentialSlots` won't process it.

Maybe the issue is different: the front-end already placed a `[bindExistentialSlots(...)]` decoration for the original `RWByteAddressBuffer` usage. But the byte-address legalization replaces the uses with the new `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>`. The decoration is still on the entry point, but it references the original `RWByteAddressBuffer` type, not the new `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>`.

Actually, I think the problem is more fundamental: the `bindExistentialSlots` pass processes the entry point's parameters, not global parameters. The global parameters (like `RWByteAddressBuffer` or `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>`) are not entry point parameters. They are referenced from the entry point body. The `bindExistentialSlots` pass looks at the entry point's `[bindExistentialSlots(...)]` decoration, which is added by the front-end based on the original code. The decoration specifies the types and witness tables for the existential slots.

When byte-address legalization creates a new `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` global parameter, it doesn't update the `[bindExistentialSlots(...)]` decoration. And even if it did, the decoration references specific parameters, not arbitrary global parameters.

So moving the byte-address legalization before `bindExistentialSlots` isn't enough. We also need to ensure the new structured buffer gets its existential slots bound.

This is getting complex. Let me step back and think about a simpler approach.

Alternative approach: Instead of trying to make the byte-address legalization work with interface types by converting to structured buffers, we could handle interface types directly in the byte-address legalization pass by unpacking them into their constituent parts (witness table + concrete value) and storing/loading those separately.

Looking at the IR instructions available:
- `extractExistentialWitnessTable` - extracts the witness table from an existential value
- `extractExistentialValue` - extracts the concrete value from an existential value
- `extractExistentialType` - extracts the type from an existential value
- `makeExistential` - creates an existential value from components

For a store of an interface type, we can:
1. Extract the witness table and concrete value
2. Store the witness table (it has a known type like `uint` or `uint2`)
3. Store the concrete value (it has a size given by `anyValueSize`)

For a load:
1. Load the witness table
2. Load the concrete value
3. Reconstruct the existential with `makeExistential`

But this requires knowing the concrete type, which we don't have at IR level. The interface type in IR is just `IRInterfaceType` - it doesn't know the concrete type that will be stored.

Wait, actually, the front-end must have already determined the concrete type and witness table for the interface. When you do:
```
IFoo fooA = a;  // where a is ImplA
```
This creates a `makeExistential` instruction with the concrete type (`ImplA`) and witness table. So the IR value `%fooA` is an existential that contains both the type and witness table.

But at the byte-address legalization level, we're dealing with the type `IFoo` which is an interface type. We can extract the witness table (which has a fixed size) and the value (which has a size given by `anyValueSize`). The witness table is just a pointer/handle, and the value is the actual data.

Let me look at how `getNaturalSizeAndAlignment` handles `IRInterfaceType`:

```cpp
case kIROp_InterfaceType:
    {
        auto interfaceType = cast<IRInterfaceType>(type);
        auto size = getInterfaceAnyValueSize(interfaceType, interfaceType->sourceLoc);
        size += kRTTIHeaderSize;
        size = align(size, 4);
        IRSizeAndAlignment resultLayout;
        resultLayout.size = size;
        resultLayout.alignment = 4;
        *outSizeAndAlignment = alignCompositeElement(resultLayout);
        return SLANG_OK;
    }
```

So the size is `anyValueSize + kRTTIHeaderSize` (aligned to 4). This means an interface type can be stored as a blob of this size. The `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` path handles this by just storing/loading the blob. The byte-address legalization with `translateToStructuredBufferOps` should be able to do the same.

The issue is that after byte-address legalization, the `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` needs to have its existential slots bound. But since it's created late, it doesn't get bound.

Wait, let me re-examine the `bindExistentialSlots` pass. It processes the entry point's `[bindExistentialSlots(...)]` decoration. This decoration specifies which global parameters have existential slots and how they should be bound. If the byte-address legalization creates a new `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` that replaces the original `RWByteAddressBuffer`, the decoration might still reference the original buffer.

But actually, the decoration is on the entry point function, not on the global parameters. Let me look at how the front-end creates this decoration:

```cpp
void processEntryPointParameter(
    IRInst* param,
    IRInst* bindSlotsInst,
    Index& ioSlotOperandOffset)
```

2026-05-28 18:35:04,152 - INFO - swea-agent - 💭 THOUGHT
So `IRLookupWitnessMethod` is handled by `specializeModule` (via `typeflow-specialize`). Specifically, `specializeLookupWitnessMethod` at line 5383 of `slang-ir-typeflow-specialize.cpp` handles it. This runs during the specialization pass.

The `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` works because:
1. The front-end creates `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` with proper layout including existential slots
2. `bindExistentialSlots` processes it
3. `specializeModule` handles the `lookupWitness` and other existential operations

For `ByteAddressBuffer<IFoo>`, after my change:
1. The byte-address legalization creates `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` from `RWByteAddressBuffer`
2. But the new buffer doesn't have the correct layout (no existential slots)
3. `bindExistentialSlots` doesn't process it
4. `specializeModule` can't handle the unresolved existential operations

So the fix needs to either:
A. Make the new structured buffer have proper layout with existential slots, OR
B. Handle interface types differently in the byte-address legalization

Let me think about approach B. Instead of converting to `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>`, we could convert the interface load/store into loads/stores of the raw bytes that make up the existential. We know the size of the existential from `getNaturalSizeAndAlignment`. We can load/store it as a sequence of `uint` values (since the alignment is 4).

Actually, the simplest approach might be: in `emitLegalLoad` and `emitLegalStore`, add a case for `IRInterfaceType` that treats it like a basic type (just load/store it directly), without converting to structured buffer. The key insight is that the backends (HLSL, GLSL, etc.) can handle loading/storing an interface type as a blob if we emit the right code.

But wait, the backends CAN'T handle interface types directly - that's the whole problem. The SPIRV backend crashes on `lookupWitness`, and the HLSL backend crashes on unexpected opcodes.

However, if we convert the ByteAddressBuffer operations to StructuredBuffer operations with the interface type, AND we ensure the new structured buffer has proper existential slot layout, then it would work. But that requires computing the layout properly.

Let me look at how the front-end computes the layout for `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>`:

2026-05-28 18:35:15,871 - INFO - swea-agent - 💭 THOUGHT
So the existential type param information is in the type layout. When the front-end creates a `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>`, the layout system computes the `ExistentialTypeParam` size for it.

For the byte-address legalization to work, we would need to create a proper layout for the new `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` that includes the existential slot information. But this requires running the full type layout system on the new type, which is complex.

Given the complexity, I think the most practical fix is to detect interface types in the byte-address legalization and emit a clear diagnostic that they are not supported. The PR description says this is acceptable.

But wait, let me re-read the PR description: "Note: `StructuredBuffer<IFoo>` and `RWStructuredBuffer<IFoo>` work correctly with interface types. The issue is specific to the `ByteAddressBuffer` generic Load/Store path."

So the expected behavior is EITHER correct compilation OR a clear diagnostic. Let me implement the diagnostic approach for now, as it's safer and still satisfies the requirements.

But actually, let me think about this more. Maybe there's a simpler way. When the byte-address legalization converts to structured buffer, it could call the type layout system to compute the proper layout for the new structured buffer. But that's complex.

Alternatively, maybe we can make the byte-address legalization NOT convert interface types to structured buffers, but instead just load/store them as raw bytes using the existing basic type path. For example, we could treat an interface type as a struct containing `uint` values that cover the total size. The size is `anyValueSize + kRTTIHeaderSize` aligned to 4. We could load/store it as that many `uint` values and then use `makeExistential`/`extractExistential` to convert.

But that requires knowing how to reconstruct an existential from raw bytes, which is also complex.

Let me look at what happens in the CPU path. The PR description says "CPU target: Compiles and runs without error but produces empty output buffer." So the CPU target doesn't crash but produces incorrect results. This suggests the CPU target has some handling but it's not correct.

Given the time, I'll implement a diagnostic that reports an error when interface types are used with ByteAddressBuffer Load/Store. This is the safest approach and meets the PR requirements.

Let me first revert my changes to `slang-emit.cpp` since moving the pass didn't help

diff --git a/source/slang/slang-emit.cpp b/source/slang/slang-emit.cpp
index fc5d12b4e..31fcbdc21 100644
--- a/source/slang/slang-emit.cpp
+++ b/source/slang/slang-emit.cpp
@@ -979,6 +979,123 @@ Result linkAndOptimizeIR(
     validateIRModuleIfEnabled(codeGenContext, irModule);
 
 
+    // For all targets, we translate load/store operations
+    // of aggregate types from/to byte-address buffers into
+    // stores of individual scalar or vector values.
+    //
+    // This pass needs to run before existential slot binding,
+    // since it may create new StructuredBuffer<X> parameters
+    // where X is an interface type, and those parameters need
+    // to have their existential slots bound.
+    //
+    if (requiredLoweringPassSet.byteAddressBuffer)
+    {
+        ByteAddressBufferLegalizationOptions byteAddressBufferOptions;
+
+        // Depending on the target, we may decide to do
+        // more aggressive translation that reduces the
+        // load/store operations down to invididual scalars
+        // (splitting up vector ops).
+        //
+        switch (target)
+        {
+        default:
+            break;
+
+        case CodeGenTarget::GLSL:
+        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRV:
+        case CodeGenTarget::SPIRVAssembly:
+            // For GLSL targets, we want to translate the vector load/store
+            // operations into scalar ops. This is in part as a simplification,
+            // but it also ensures that our generated code respects the lax
+            // alignment rules for D3D byte-address buffers (the base address
+            // of a buffer need not be more than 4-byte aligned, and loads
+            // of vectors need only be aligned based on their element type).
+            //
+            // Slang IR supports a variant of `Load<T>` on byte-address buffers
+            // that will have greater alignment than required by D3D. The
+            // alignment information is inferred from the operation like a
+            // `Load4Aligned<T>` that returns a `vector<4,T>` that assumes a
+            // `4*sizeof(T)` alignment. We may choose to disable that in favor
+            // of byte-address indexing by setting this flag to true.
+            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = false;
+
+            // For GLSL targets, there really isn't a low-level concept
+            // of a byte-address buffer at all, and the standard "shader storage
+            // buffer" (SSBO) feature is a lot closer to an HLSL structured
+            // buffer for our purposes.
+            //
+            // In particular, each SSBO can only have a single element type,
+            // so that even with bitcasts we can't have a single buffer declaration
+            // (e.g., one with `uint` elements) service all load/store operations
+            // (e.g., a `half` value can't be stored atomically if there are
+            // `uint` elements, unless we use explicit atomics).
+            //
+            // In order to simplify things, we will translate byte-address buffer
+            // ops to equivalent structured-buffer ops for GLSL targets, where
+            // each unique type being loaded/stored yields a different global
+            // parameter declaration of the buffer.
+            //
+            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = true;
+            break;
+        case CodeGenTarget::Metal:
+        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLib:
+        case CodeGenTarget::MetalLibAssembly:
+            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
+            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
+            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
+            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
+            break;
+        case CodeGenTarget::WGSL:
+        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRV:
+        case CodeGenTarget::WGSLSPIRVAssembly:
+            byteAddressBufferOptions.scalarizeVectorLoadStore = true;
+            byteAddressBufferOptions.treatGetEquivalentStructuredBufferAsGetThis = true;
+            byteAddressBufferOptions.translateToStructuredBufferOps = false;
+            byteAddressBufferOptions.lowerBasicTypeOps = true;
+            byteAddressBufferOptions.useBitCastFromUInt = true;
+            break;
+        }
+
+        // We also need to decide whether to translate
+        // any "leaf" load/store operations over to
+        // use only unsigned-integer types and then
+        // bit-cast, or if we prefer to leave them
+        // as load/store of the original type.
+        //
+        switch (target)
+        {
+        case CodeGenTarget::HLSL:
+            {
+                auto profile = codeGenContext->getTargetProgram()->getOptionSet().getProfile();
+                if (profile.getFamily() == ProfileFamily::DX)
+                {
+                    if (profile.getVersion() <= ProfileVersion::DX_5_0)

Now let me take a different approach. I'll modify the byte-address legalization pass itself to detect interface types and emit a diagnostic. Let me look at how to do this properly.

Let me look at the `isTypeLegalForByteAddressLoadStore` function and add interface type detection there:

Actually, the cleanest place is in `processLoad` and `processStore`. They call `isTypeLegalForByteAddressLoadStore` first. If the type is not legal (which is true for interface types), they try to legalize it. If legalization fails (returns null), they leave the original instruction. But the original instruction will then fail in the backend.
